title: Enterprise Architecture & Integration Styles
summary: How systems are structured and connected at scale, from SOA and microservices to the classic integration styles and modernization patterns embassies use to evolve legacy platforms.
content:
An embassy rarely runs one system; it runs a consular booking platform, a visa case manager, an HR system, a finance ledger, and a document archive that all must exchange data. Architecture decides how those parts are sliced and wired. A monolith packages all functionality in one deployable unit: simple to build and deploy, but a single change forces a full redeploy and one bug can take everything down. SOA (service-oriented architecture) breaks capability into coarse-grained, reusable services, historically connected through an enterprise service bus. Microservices push this further into many small, independently deployable services, each owning its own data and communicating over the network.

Microservices buy independent deployment, team autonomy, and targeted scaling, but at the cost of distributed-systems complexity: network failures, eventual consistency, harder debugging, and operational overhead. The honest default is to start with a well-structured monolith and extract services only when a clear seam and a real scaling or team-boundary pressure appear.

Domain-driven design (DDD) gives the vocabulary for drawing those seams. A bounded context is a boundary within which a model and its terms have one precise meaning; the word 'applicant' may mean something different in the visa context than in the HR context, and that is fine as long as each context is explicit. Bounded contexts map naturally onto service boundaries, and the ubiquitous language keeps code and business conversation aligned.

Regardless of style, integration between systems happens through one of four classic styles (Hohpe & Woolf). File transfer: one system writes a file, another reads it later — simple, decoupled, but stale and batch-oriented. Shared database: systems read and write the same tables — fast and consistent but tightly coupled and brittle to schema change. Remote procedure invocation (RPC): one system calls another synchronously like a local function — immediate but creates temporal coupling, since the caller waits and fails if the callee is down. Messaging: systems exchange asynchronous messages through a channel — loosely coupled and resilient, but eventually consistent and harder to reason about.

When a business process spans services, coordination is either orchestration or choreography. Orchestration uses a central conductor (often a BPM or workflow engine like Camunda or a state machine) that explicitly invokes each step and tracks progress — easy to monitor and change, but the orchestrator becomes a focal point. Choreography has each service react to events and emit its own, with no central brain — highly decoupled and scalable, but the end-to-end flow is implicit and harder to trace.

Legacy modernization rarely happens as a big-bang rewrite. The strangler fig pattern wraps the old system, routes a slice of traffic to a new implementation, and grows the new system feature by feature until the old one can be retired — named after the vine that gradually envelops its host tree. To stop a clean new system from being polluted by a messy legacy model, an anti-corruption layer (ACL) sits between them and translates between the two models, so the legacy's quirks never leak inward.

Topologies describe the wiring shape. Point-to-point connects each system directly to each other — fine for a handful of systems but explodes into an unmaintainable mesh (n² connections) as they multiply. Hub-and-spoke routes everything through a central hub that handles transformation and routing, cutting the connection count but creating a single point of failure. The bus (ESB) is a distributed backbone every system plugs into. Modern integration increasingly uses iPaaS (integration platform as a service) — cloud-hosted integration with prebuilt connectors — and API-led / composable architecture, where reusable, well-governed APIs (system, process, and experience layers) become building blocks that new applications compose rather than rebuild.

• ETL/batch integration moves large volumes on a schedule (nightly extracts), while real-time/streaming integration propagates changes continuously as events occur; the choice depends on freshness needs versus cost and complexity.
keyPoints: Monolith = one deployable; SOA = coarse reusable services on a bus; microservices = many small independently deployable services each owning its data.
Microservices trade simplicity for autonomy and scaling but add distributed-systems complexity — start monolithic and extract services only when a real seam appears.
A bounded context (DDD) is a boundary within which model terms have one precise meaning; bounded contexts map onto service boundaries.
The four integration styles are file transfer, shared database, RPC, and messaging — increasing in decoupling and resilience, decreasing in immediacy/consistency.
Shared database is the most tightly coupled style; messaging is the most loosely coupled but eventually consistent.
Orchestration uses a central conductor (BPM/workflow engine) for explicit, observable flow; choreography uses event reactions with no central brain — decoupled but implicit.
Strangler fig incrementally replaces a legacy system by routing slices of traffic to a new one until the old can be retired, avoiding a risky big-bang rewrite.
An anti-corruption layer translates between a new model and a legacy model so the legacy's quirks don't pollute the new system.
Point-to-point scales as n² connections; hub-and-spoke centralizes routing but is a single point of failure; a bus is a shared backbone.
iPaaS is cloud integration-as-a-service; API-led/composable architecture builds applications from reusable, governed APIs (system/process/experience layers).

flashcards.front: Monolith vs microservices in one line each
flashcards.back: Monolith: all functionality in one deployable unit — simple but couples everything. Microservices: many small, independently deployable services, each owning its data — autonomous but distributed-complexity heavy.

flashcards.front: What is SOA and how does it classically connect services?
flashcards.back: Service-oriented architecture decomposes capability into coarse-grained, reusable services, historically connected through an enterprise service bus (ESB).

flashcards.front: What is a bounded context in DDD?
flashcards.back: A boundary within which a domain model and its terms have a single, precise meaning; the same word can mean different things in different contexts. Bounded contexts map naturally onto service boundaries.

flashcards.front: Name the four classic integration styles
flashcards.back: File transfer, shared database, remote procedure invocation (RPC), and messaging.

flashcards.front: Which integration style is most tightly coupled, and which most loosely coupled?
flashcards.back: Shared database is the most tightly coupled (systems share schema); messaging is the most loosely coupled (asynchronous channels).

flashcards.front: What coupling problem does synchronous RPC introduce?
flashcards.back: Temporal coupling — the caller blocks waiting for the callee and fails if the callee is down or slow; both must be available at the same time.

flashcards.front: Orchestration vs choreography
flashcards.back: Orchestration: a central conductor explicitly invokes and tracks each step (easy to monitor, central focal point). Choreography: services react to events and emit their own, no central brain (decoupled but the flow is implicit).

flashcards.front: What does a BPM / workflow engine do?
flashcards.back: Coordinates multi-step business processes as explicit, observable, often long-running flows — acting as the orchestrator (e.g., Camunda, a state machine).

flashcards.front: What is the strangler fig pattern?
flashcards.back: Incremental modernization: wrap the legacy system, route slices of traffic to a new implementation, and grow it feature by feature until the legacy can be retired — avoiding a big-bang rewrite.

flashcards.front: What is an anti-corruption layer (ACL)?
flashcards.back: A translation layer between a new system and a legacy one that converts between their models, preventing the legacy's quirks and bad model from leaking into the new system.

flashcards.front: Why does point-to-point integration not scale?
flashcards.back: The number of direct connections grows roughly as n², producing an unmaintainable mesh as the number of systems increases.

flashcards.front: Hub-and-spoke vs bus topology
flashcards.back: Hub-and-spoke routes all traffic through a central hub for transformation/routing (fewer connections, single point of failure). A bus (ESB) is a distributed backbone every system plugs into.

flashcards.front: What is iPaaS?
flashcards.back: Integration Platform as a Service — cloud-hosted integration with prebuilt connectors, managed runtime, and tooling, so teams build integrations without running their own middleware.

flashcards.front: What is API-led / composable architecture?
flashcards.back: Designing reusable, governed APIs — often layered as system, process, and experience APIs — so new applications are composed from existing building blocks rather than rebuilt.

flashcards.front: ETL/batch vs real-time integration
flashcards.back: Batch/ETL moves large volumes on a schedule (e.g., nightly extracts); real-time/streaming propagates changes continuously as events. Choose by freshness needs vs cost and complexity.

title: Data Persistence & State for Integration
summary: How integrated systems store, cache, lock, and keep data consistent — covering relational vs NoSQL, the dual-write problem and outbox pattern, locking, message dedup, schema registries, and master data management.
content:
Every integration eventually has to decide where data lives and how its state stays correct when multiple systems touch it. The first choice is relational versus NoSQL. Relational databases (PostgreSQL, Oracle) enforce schemas, relationships, and ACID transactions — ideal for consular records and finance where correctness and complex queries matter. NoSQL spans document, key-value, column-family, and graph stores; they trade strict schema and joins for horizontal scale, flexible structure, and high write throughput — useful for event logs, caches, and large denormalized read models. For integration the rule of thumb is: relational for systems of record and transactional integrity, NoSQL for scale, flexible payloads, and read-optimized projections.

Databases are expensive to connect to, so connection pooling keeps a fixed set of reusable open connections rather than opening one per request. This caps load on the database, reduces latency, and protects it from connection exhaustion under spikes — a pool that is too small throttles throughput, while one too large can overwhelm the database.

Caching reduces load and latency by keeping hot data closer to the application, often in Redis or Memcached. In the cache-aside pattern the application checks the cache first, and on a miss loads from the database and populates the cache; in write-through the application writes to the cache and database together, keeping them consistent at the cost of slower writes. A TTL (time-to-live) expires entries so stale data eventually clears. The hard part of caching is invalidation — deciding when cached data no longer reflects the source of truth.

The sharpest integration hazard is the dual-write problem: when a single operation must update a database and also publish a message (or call another system), and the two are not in one transaction, a crash between them leaves the systems inconsistent — the row is saved but the event is lost, or vice versa. The fix is the transactional outbox pattern: within the same database transaction, write the business change and an event row to an outbox table; a separate relay then reads the outbox and publishes the events. Because the write and the outbox row commit atomically, no event is ever lost. The inbox pattern is the mirror image on the consumer side: record processed message IDs so duplicates are ignored.

Keeping data consistent across systems is a spectrum. Distributed transactions (two-phase commit) try to make multiple resources commit atomically, but they are slow, lock resources, and scale poorly across services — generally avoided in modern integration. The pragmatic alternative is eventual consistency: each system updates independently and converges over time through events, accepting a window where systems briefly disagree.

Within a single database, concurrent updates are managed by locking. Pessimistic locking takes a lock before reading/writing so others must wait — safe under high contention but it blocks and risks deadlocks. Optimistic locking takes no lock; it reads a version number, and on write checks the version is unchanged, failing and retrying if someone else changed it first — better for low-contention, high-read workloads.

In messaging, duplicates and reordering are facts of life. Most brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery, so consumers must be idempotent and often deduplicate using a unique message ID and an inbox of seen IDs. Strict ordering usually holds only within a partition or key, so designs that need ordering route related messages to the same key.

Finally, integrated systems must agree on the shape and meaning of data. A schema registry stores and versions the schemas (Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema) that producers and consumers share, enforcing compatibility rules so a producer cannot ship a breaking change that silently corrupts consumers — these schemas are the data contracts. Above individual records, master data management (MDM) establishes one authoritative source for core shared entities (a person, an organization, a country), while reference data manages the controlled value lists (currency codes, country codes, status enumerations) every system reuses, preventing the chaos of each system inventing its own version of the truth.
keyPoints: Relational databases give schema + ACID for systems of record; NoSQL trades joins/strict schema for horizontal scale and flexible, high-throughput storage.
Connection pooling reuses a bounded set of DB connections to cap load and latency; too small throttles, too large can overwhelm the database.
Cache-aside: app checks cache, loads from DB on miss and populates it. Write-through: writes go to cache and DB together. TTL expires entries; invalidation is the hard problem.
The dual-write problem: updating a DB and publishing a message without one transaction can lose the event or the data on a crash.
The transactional outbox writes the business change and an event row in one DB transaction, then a relay publishes the events — guaranteeing no event is lost.
The inbox pattern records processed message IDs on the consumer side to discard duplicates (deduplication).
Distributed transactions (2PC) are slow and scale poorly across services; eventual consistency via events is the modern default.
Pessimistic locking blocks others up front (safe under contention, risks deadlock); optimistic locking checks a version on write and retries on conflict (good for low contention).
At-least-once delivery means consumers must be idempotent and deduplicate; strict ordering typically holds only within a partition/key.
A schema registry versions shared schemas (Avro/Protobuf/JSON Schema) and enforces compatibility — these are the data contracts between producers and consumers.
Master data management defines one authoritative source for core shared entities; reference data manages controlled value lists (country/currency/status codes).

flashcards.front: When relational vs when NoSQL for integration?
flashcards.back: Relational (ACID, schema, joins) for systems of record and transactional integrity. NoSQL (document/key-value/column/graph) for horizontal scale, flexible payloads, and read-optimized projections.

flashcards.front: What does connection pooling solve?
flashcards.back: Opening a DB connection per request is expensive. A pool keeps a bounded set of reusable open connections, capping DB load, cutting latency, and preventing connection exhaustion under spikes.

flashcards.front: Cache-aside vs write-through
flashcards.back: Cache-aside: app reads cache, on miss loads from DB and populates cache. Write-through: app writes to cache and DB together, keeping them consistent at the cost of slower writes.

flashcards.front: What is a TTL in caching, and what's the hard problem?
flashcards.back: Time-to-live expires cache entries after a set duration so stale data eventually clears. The hard problem is invalidation — knowing when cached data no longer matches the source of truth.

flashcards.front: What is the dual-write problem?
flashcards.back: When one operation must update the database AND publish a message/call another system without a shared transaction, a crash between the two leaves systems inconsistent — the data saves but the event is lost, or vice versa.

flashcards.front: How does the transactional outbox pattern fix dual-write?
flashcards.back: In the same DB transaction, write the business change and an event row to an outbox table. A separate relay reads the outbox and publishes events. Atomic commit means no event is ever lost.

flashcards.front: What is the inbox pattern?
flashcards.back: The consumer-side mirror of the outbox: record processed message IDs so re-delivered duplicates are detected and ignored (idempotent consumption).

flashcards.front: Why are distributed transactions (2PC) usually avoided across services?
flashcards.back: They hold locks, are slow, reduce availability, and scale poorly across networks/services. Eventual consistency through events is the pragmatic alternative.

flashcards.front: What is eventual consistency?
flashcards.back: Each system updates independently and converges over time via propagated events, accepting a temporary window where systems disagree — in exchange for availability and scale.

flashcards.front: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking
flashcards.back: Pessimistic: lock before access so others wait (safe under high contention, can deadlock). Optimistic: no lock; check a version number on write and retry if it changed (better for low-contention, read-heavy workloads).

flashcards.front: Why must message consumers be idempotent?
flashcards.back: Most brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery, so the same message can arrive more than once. Idempotent processing (often with a dedup ID/inbox) ensures duplicates don't cause double effects.

flashcards.front: Where does strict message ordering typically hold?
flashcards.back: Only within a partition or key, not globally. Designs needing ordering route related messages to the same partition key.

flashcards.front: What is a schema registry and why use one?
flashcards.back: A central store that versions shared schemas (Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema) and enforces compatibility rules, so producers can't ship breaking changes that silently corrupt consumers. The schemas act as data contracts.

flashcards.front: What is a data contract?
flashcards.back: An explicit, versioned agreement on the structure, types, and semantics of exchanged data between a producer and its consumers — often enforced via a schema registry.

flashcards.front: Master data management vs reference data
flashcards.back: MDM establishes one authoritative source for core shared entities (person, organization, country). Reference data manages controlled value lists (currency, country, status codes) reused across systems.

title: API Management, Gateways & Operational Integration
summary: The operational layer that runs integrations in production — API gateways, lifecycle and developer portals, OpenAPI specs, service discovery, load balancing, safe deployments, observability, error handling, security, and SLAs.
content:
Once APIs exist, something has to manage them in production, and that something is usually an API gateway. The gateway is the single front door between clients and backend services. Its responsibilities include routing requests to the right service, authenticating and authorizing callers, rate limiting and throttling to protect backends, enforcing quotas per client, transforming requests and responses (protocol or format), and caching responses. Centralizing these cross-cutting concerns means each backend service doesn't reimplement them.

Rate limiting and quotas are distinct: rate limiting caps requests per short window (e.g., 100 per minute) to smooth load and stop abuse, often via token-bucket or leaky-bucket algorithms, while a quota caps total usage over a longer period (e.g., 10,000 per day) usually tied to a subscription tier. Throttling is the act of rejecting or delaying requests once a limit is hit, typically returning HTTP 429 with a Retry-After hint so well-behaved clients back off.

APIs have a lifecycle: design, publish, version, deprecate, and retire. A developer portal is where consumers discover APIs, read documentation, get credentials, and try endpoints — turning an API into a usable product. The documentation backbone is the OpenAPI specification (formerly Swagger), a machine-readable description of endpoints, parameters, schemas, and responses. Because it is machine-readable, OpenAPI drives auto-generated docs, client SDKs, server stubs, mock servers, and request validation from one source of truth.

In a dynamic environment, service instances come and go, so clients can't rely on fixed addresses. Service discovery solves this: instances register with a service registry (e.g., Consul, Eureka) and callers look up healthy instances by name. Load balancing then spreads traffic across those instances — round-robin, least-connections, or weighted — and uses health checks to detect and remove unhealthy instances so traffic only goes to working ones.

Deploying changes to live APIs safely uses progressive strategies. Blue-green keeps two identical environments and switches all traffic from the old (blue) to the new (green) at once, enabling instant rollback by switching back. Canary releases route a small percentage of traffic to the new version first, watch metrics, and ramp up only if it's healthy — limiting blast radius. Both depend on backward-compatible API changes so old and new can coexist during the transition.

You cannot operate what you cannot see, so observability rests on three pillars: logs (discrete events), metrics (aggregated numbers like latency and error rate), and traces (the path of a single request across services). The glue is the correlation ID (or trace ID) — a unique identifier attached to a request at the edge and propagated through every downstream call and message, so a single transaction can be reconstructed end-to-end across systems.

Things will fail, so error handling must be deliberate. Transient failures get retries with backoff; persistent failures must not loop forever. A poison message — one that repeatedly fails processing — is detected by a retry/redelivery count and moved to a dead-letter queue for inspection instead of blocking the queue. Responses should use correct status codes and structured error bodies so callers can react programmatically.

Security at this layer is layered. TLS encrypts traffic in transit; mTLS additionally authenticates both client and server with certificates for service-to-service trust. OAuth scopes restrict what a token is allowed to do, enforcing least privilege. Input validation at the gateway and service rejects malformed or malicious payloads, and a WAF (web application firewall) filters common attacks like injection and cross-site scripting before they reach services.

Finally, integrations are governed by SLAs (service-level agreements) — formal commitments on availability, latency, and throughput, underpinned by measurable SLOs and SLIs. To honor them under load, systems apply backpressure: signaling upstream to slow down (via 429s, bounded queues, or flow control) rather than accepting more work than they can handle and collapsing. Backpressure plus throttling, retries with backoff, and dead-lettering together keep an integration resilient when demand exceeds capacity.
keyPoints: An API gateway is the single front door handling routing, authn/authz, rate limiting/throttling, quotas, request/response transformation, and caching — so backends don't reimplement them.
Rate limit = requests per short window; quota = total usage over a longer period (often per tier); throttling = rejecting/delaying over-limit requests, usually with HTTP 429 + Retry-After.
The API lifecycle is design → publish → version → deprecate → retire; a developer portal lets consumers discover, document, get credentials, and try APIs.
OpenAPI/Swagger is a machine-readable API spec that drives generated docs, client SDKs, server stubs, mocks, and request validation from one source of truth.
Service discovery via a registry lets instances register and callers look up healthy instances by name instead of hardcoding addresses.
Load balancing spreads traffic (round-robin, least-connections, weighted) and uses health checks to route only to healthy instances.
Blue-green switches all traffic between two environments for instant rollback; canary shifts a small traffic percentage first and ramps up if healthy — both need backward-compatible changes.
Observability rests on logs, metrics, and traces; a correlation/trace ID propagated across calls reconstructs one transaction end-to-end.
A poison message repeatedly fails processing and is routed to a dead-letter queue after a retry-count threshold so it doesn't block the queue.
Security is layered: TLS in transit, mTLS for mutual service authentication, OAuth scopes for least privilege, input validation, and a WAF against injection/XSS.
SLAs commit to availability/latency/throughput (backed by SLOs/SLIs); backpressure signals upstream to slow down rather than overloading and collapsing.

flashcards.front: What are the core responsibilities of an API gateway?
flashcards.back: It's the single front door: routing, authentication/authorization, rate limiting/throttling, quota enforcement, request/response transformation, and caching — centralizing cross-cutting concerns away from backend services.

flashcards.front: Rate limiting vs quota
flashcards.back: Rate limiting caps requests per short window (e.g., 100/min) to smooth load and stop abuse. A quota caps total usage over a longer period (e.g., 10,000/day), usually tied to a subscription tier.

flashcards.front: What is throttling and what HTTP status signals it?
flashcards.back: Throttling is rejecting or delaying requests once a limit is exceeded. It typically returns HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests), often with a Retry-After header so clients back off.

flashcards.front: What is a developer portal?
flashcards.back: A self-service site where API consumers discover APIs, read documentation, obtain credentials/keys, and try endpoints — turning an API into a consumable product.

flashcards.front: What is OpenAPI (Swagger) and why does it matter?
flashcards.back: A machine-readable specification of an API's endpoints, parameters, schemas, and responses. Being machine-readable, it generates docs, client SDKs, server stubs, mock servers, and request validation from one source of truth.

flashcards.front: What problem does service discovery solve?
flashcards.back: In dynamic environments instances change address, so clients can't hardcode locations. Instances register with a service registry and callers look up healthy instances by logical name.

flashcards.front: What do health checks do in load balancing?
flashcards.back: They probe each instance's health so the load balancer routes traffic only to healthy instances and removes failing ones from rotation.

flashcards.front: Blue-green vs canary deployment
flashcards.back: Blue-green keeps two identical environments and switches all traffic at once (instant rollback by switching back). Canary routes a small traffic percentage to the new version first and ramps up if metrics stay healthy.

flashcards.front: What are the three pillars of observability?
flashcards.back: Logs (discrete events), metrics (aggregated numbers like latency/error rate), and traces (the path of one request across services).

flashcards.front: What is a correlation ID (trace ID)?
flashcards.back: A unique identifier assigned to a request at the edge and propagated through every downstream call and message, so one transaction can be reconstructed end-to-end across systems.

flashcards.front: What is a poison message and how is it handled?
flashcards.back: A message that repeatedly fails processing. After a retry/redelivery threshold it's moved to a dead-letter queue for inspection so it doesn't block the queue or loop forever.

flashcards.front: TLS vs mTLS
flashcards.back: TLS encrypts traffic and authenticates the server to the client. mTLS (mutual TLS) additionally authenticates the client to the server via certificates — used for service-to-service trust.

flashcards.front: What do OAuth scopes enforce?
flashcards.back: Least privilege — a scope restricts what an access token is permitted to do (e.g., read-only vs write), so a token can't exceed its granted permissions.

flashcards.front: What is a WAF?
flashcards.back: A web application firewall that filters and blocks common web attacks (SQL injection, cross-site scripting, etc.) before they reach the application or service.

flashcards.front: What is backpressure and why is it needed?
flashcards.back: A mechanism to signal upstream producers to slow down (via 429s, bounded queues, flow control) when a system nears capacity — so it sheds or paces load instead of accepting more than it can handle and collapsing.

flashcards.front: What is an SLA, and what underpins it?
flashcards.back: A service-level agreement is a formal commitment on availability, latency, and throughput. It's underpinned by SLOs (objectives) and SLIs (measured indicators) that quantify whether the commitment is met.
